sentences.text: There was a lot of times in Bialystok, [INAUDIBLE] a Jewish city.
My name is Michael Lipenholtz.
L-I-P-E-N-H-O-L-T-Z.
Michael, Mikhoel.
Lipenholtz.
In Vilna.
This was then in Poland.
Later, it was a Russian, and Lithuanian.
And they used to fight over it.
I forgot.
[LAUGHS]
November.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] November.
[INAUDIBLE]
How old it makes me today?
[LAUGHS] I forgot.
I don't think-- I got a wife-- I'm not allowed to talk, no?
Yes.
Yes.
I always went to school.
It wasn't such a thing, like no school.
The name it used to-- they used to call then, Gymnasium [NON-ENGLISH] Vilna.
This is what is the elementary school, too.
A Hebrew school.
Real Hebrew, you know.
The street?
[NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH] A.
My parents.
Chaim.
Sonia, Shaina.
Bisciks.
My brother.
Moshe.
He was born [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '11, I think, '11, 1911.
He used to be in business.
And then later on I remember after the-- the Depression, he had to come-- worked with his mother-- with my mother in the store.
A grocery store.
Yes.
[LAUGHS] What-- I was-- sometimes I was helping them.
You know, on-- on Sunday, you had to have your stores closed.
You're not allowed to have them open.
So they used to do the business through the back store.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was on a Sunday.
Yes.
And I-- on Saturday, on Saturday, they were sleeping or resting or do something.
I was standing over there on Saturday.
Orthodox, sure.
Yes.
Yes.
Shabbos was a real Shabbos.
Nothing doing.
What did I eat Friday night?
Oh, yeah.
We have--
Challah they used bake themselves.
My mother used to come home and bake-- bake the challah.
They [INAUDIBLE]-- everybody had a maid.
If you worked in-- in any place, you had to have a maid.
Because a maid used to cost you 20 zlotys. 20 zlotys was $5 a month.
[LAUGHS] Rosalia.
She--
No, wasn't Jewish.
She used to take me to church [LAUGHS] on Sunday.
No.
When we find out, they had to-- had to fire the maid.
But she took me every time.
[INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
Yes.
We had to have a maid.
We couldn't have it without the maid, you know.
But you had to-- to cook for Shabbos.
They used to-- they didn't trust the maid.
My mother used to cook, come home.
Yes.
I didn't eat it.
But I didn't like fish.
Well, chicken, meat, and everything. 'Cause, no problem the food, by us anyway.
Sure.
Sometimes.
They had friends most of the time.
Because my father was born-- oh, boy-- in [NON-ENGLISH].
This was a city not far from Vilna, a mid-sized city.
So they used to come to Vilna.
They used to come to Chaim Lipenholtz.
They used to sleep-- we had a special place for-- for-- for company.
What did your house look like?
My huh?
No, we had an apartment.
[INAUDIBLE] a four-room apartment, I think.
And once we had a five room apartment.
Yes.
Yes.
We had these things.
Oh, yes.
I was sitting in the front of the store.
It was big street, you know, like.
And all of a sudden, somebody hit me like with a hammer, you know.
And I-- I-- and I was stand up.
And I was looking, like didn't see who it was.
But this wasn't Jewish.
You see, they hated the Jews.
That's what it was.
And I was always with-- with them.
I went to the army, I was the one.
Jew in this-- in this part, you know, in the-- in the [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, how they call it?
They used to called it [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
[NON-ENGLISH], it was like a-- for-- for-- anyway, I was one Jew in this place.
I was lucky, because there was another guy from-- from-- from Poland, you know, from far Poland, from the back.
They speak-- spoke a good Polish.
But I looked like a goy.
And I-- so they didn't know.
Who is the Jew?
I went and-- and then I went to the school [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of [INAUDIBLE]-- how is it?
When you finished, you became a sergeant or something like this.
Engineer Engineer.
Was it engineering school?
No, no, no.
This wasn't engineer.
Only school.
Military school.
But they hate the Jews.
And lately, before the war if you had finished school with-- you could get in in college. if they could do it.
They wouldn't take no Jewish at all.
They wouldn't take Jewishes in the army.
This was the-- they didn't talk about it.
But they didn't took it.
But the guys who had the schooling, were lucky.
But they didn't take them into their army.
My mother?
When you were a young boy, yes.
She was a young girl, a healthy one.
That's it.
My father used to go to shul every day.
Shabbos?
I think so, maybe not.
I don't remember.
What?
Oh, Pesach, was a seder.
Sure.
This was normal.
It wasn't a holiday, you know, like to make a-- because this was like this.
Pesach was real Pescach.
We used to buy-- bake the matzoh.
We had to go to the bakery.
There used to be a bakery.
And they made it kosher for Pesach.
And you used to bring the flour to bake the matzoh.
And you were waiting and watching.
Not oven, after.
From the oven into the basket, but it was a big basket like this.
And then you take it and carry it home.
In the bakery, yeah.
Sure.
I forgot about the wine.
Yeah, sure.
We used to buy grapes and let them for ferment.
And then you had to squeeze it, and I-- I forgot.
Everybody.
[LAUGHTER] My father was the man doer.
And we used to make [INAUDIBLE].
You know what [INAUDIBLE] is?
We used to make [INAUDIBLE], too.
It tasted good, too.
That's right.
[LAUGHTER]
Sure.
What's the matter with you?
You're asking me such questions.
[LAUGHTER] I was specialist.
[LAUGHTER]
We had to ask for it.
Ask-- I don't know I asked.
I forgot.
We used-- we used to be boys, you know.
We used-- our friends were Jewish.
And every had- everybody had a Jewish education.
And we were happy.
Do you know who was my rabbi?
[INAUDIBLE] You hear about him?
And he was the rabbi from Vilna.
I remember him.
He was a [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what that means?
They used to come from all the world he should [NON-ENGLISH].
That's the way there.
And he was-- he was the rabbi.
[BACKROUND NOISE] But he was lucky.
When the Germans got in, he died right away.
He was-- when he was working in the street, he was working with [INAUDIBLE].
You know?
He was an old man.
What?
[INAUDIBLE] Koussevitzki.
Josef.
Josef Koussevitzki?
Moshe Koussevitzki, that's right.
He-- he started over in my shul.
He started like a-- we were the third of the fourth biggest shul in Vilna.
And we had a lot of shuls, in the hundreds.
And he was started [INAUDIBLE] [NON-ENGLISH].
And they chose him.
And he was a lot of years over there.
And from-- and from there I think he went to the Dutch shul, you know, the biggest.
And from there, they took him to United States.
[INAUDIBLE] was the name.
And the guy who built the shul, the rich guy, his name was [INAUDIBLE].
A nice shul, a real nice shul even here in the United States with tall ceilings, yeah.
Women were sitting-- if they had a balcony, they were in the balcony.
If they hadn't a balcony, they had to sit in the back.
I'm sorry.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
We had a lot of newspapers.
What's happening?
We knew everything.
This was real thing.
[INAUDIBLE] maybe Vilna, like this, Jewish newspapers, had maybe six, seven newspapers, you know.
No.
Not-- not anybody had a telephone.
Telephone are for doctors.
And who needed a telephone?
You know, for bankers.
No.
I was the only one.
You know why?
Because I-- I-- I wasn't looking for girls.
[LAUGHTER] I had a lot of girls.
And I didn't [INAUDIBLE] to.
[LAUGHS]
We went to the movies.
We went to the games.
Soccer.
Yeah, I play soccer.
And I-- I have boxing, too.
When the Germans came and they moved the cadenza from the wall-- you know, they were looking for something.
They say, who was living here a boxed?
It was so many boxing gloves over there.
[LAUGHS] Really.
I'm-- I'm telling just the truth here now.
It was good for me.
I [INAUDIBLE].
Later on, became worse.
Later on, you couldn't get a job.
I had a brother five years older.
He finished gymnasium and everything.
Moshe.
And he couldn't get a Job for nothing, for no money.
A Jew couldn't get a job.
And I grow up in meantime, and I see what's going to happen.
No jobs.
You can have a job maybe to be something.
But it's not the job, you know.
So I decided myself-- because it was you had to finish gymnasium.
The rest wasn't so important by-- by a Jew, you know.
In Vilna, we had a technical school.
It was famous in the United States.
Because they paid for it, for the school.
[LAUGHS] From-- they were [INAUDIBLE] like to the [INAUDIBLE] or something like this.
And I finished that.
I had to go to the gymnasium.
My father wanted me to go over there.
The-- the price was the same almost every place.
So I-- I decide I'll go to the technical school, you know.
And then I was-- but I was there three years.
And I finished.
But I got a job right away.
I had a job in-- what was it?
[INAUDIBLE] they used to call it, a famous radio factory in the world.
So I got the job there.
What was your job?
I became a technician over there, you know.
But I knew everything, you know, mechanical.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a job.
And then I went to work for another job.
And I worked two jobs.
And it wasn't hard for me.
I don't know what it was there.
I was enjoying it.
But the meantime, I had two jobs.
And all of a sudden, I used to make money from both sides.
And I live in the big house, you know, in the big house in Vilna.
We had an elevator.
This was the second elevator [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] in Vilna.
The first one was belonged to the transportation employment, you know.
It belonged to the government.
So they had the first one.
And we had the second.
This was six stories.
We had six.
And they had seven.
[LAUGHS]
That's right.
And after the war, I took over the-- the first one.
I took it over for the-- for the [INAUDIBLE] you know.
I knew where it is.
And I knew all the [INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
They didn't take too many Jews.
I don't know.
Maybe they liked me.
[LAUGHTER] I mean it.
This was a hard job.
Then what-- what was it?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was like a year before the war.
You see, from over there they took us from Vilna down to Poland over there.
And it started, the war started.
We didn't know-- we didn't know nothing.
We didn't have no radio in the-- in the army.
I'll tell you something about the army.
Do you want to know?
[LAUGHS] Nothing good.
[LAUGHS] In the army, they took us over there.
And right away I something anti-Semitism, you know?
They hate Jews.
It was in their-- in their nature, in the blood.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So the-- after the first or second day, they wake us up in the middle of the night.
What happened?
The guy, you know, the corporal took him.
This was to take-- take something from the soldiers, you know, to break him.
He thought-- he could break his leg, he says.
It was a piece of straw like this.
And he could break his leg [INAUDIBLE].
So they wake us up.
And [INAUDIBLE] everybody up, you know, in-- in-- had a-- they didn't get no [INAUDIBLE].
Had something wrong with us, you know.
We had to take brushes and clean the-- the floors, everything.
We-- but the guys, the rest of it, not the Jews, they didn't have.
They had some-- some brushes, you know.
So they used to clean it with the-- with [INAUDIBLE].
I had-- I had a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a shoe brush, you know, to-- to brush the shoes.
Well, of course, I was doing it.
And then we started together, you know, put the-- the dirt maybe so much there together.
And we had to pull it in the pail.
So one of the guys took some dirt, put it over my head, you know.
I was [INAUDIBLE].
So I don't know what happened.
I got up.
And I had the brush in my hand.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I broke his nose.
[LAUGHS]
This was the first thing right away.
So I had respect.
All of a sudden, I became one of the boys, you know.
There had a lot of respect for me.
But they didn't put me in jail for breaking a nose.
It was really [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But it was before you would swear to the-- to the constitutionals or somebody over there that you obey all the laws.
And then-- then they can take you to the-- to the court and put you in jail.
But it was before.
So I was lucky they didn't put me in.
Yeah, everybody had a horse.
[NON-ENGLISH]
[INAUDIBLE] is a-- a gypsy.
What happened to everybody?
But-- [LAUGHS]
With the horses to the war, we put us-- they put us in train, five or six horses in a train.
And one guy who had to clean them, you know, from the-- and they took us down to Poland, you know, down.
Down to, like-- not far from the Romanian or something. frontier.
And they let us out from the trains.
And we started-- they started bombarding us.
We didn't know nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
They were already the Germans there.
And we had the horses.
And they had the-- the-- what do you call it?
The tanks.
So what should we do?
And all of a sudden, all the officers vanished.
No officers.
Nobody should take us, tell us what to do.
And we-- we had horses.
We didn't have no food.
This was the Polish army.
Help me.
And then we had to go.
We didn't have to-- we didn't know where to go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were looking for the-- for the officers.
I went with another guy.
He says, [NON-ENGLISH].
Means, if somebody goes after us, they shoot us.
And then through the night, they disappeared.
This was not just from a-- from a-- 1,000 men.
This was for 5,000 men.
They disappeared.
Nobody.
What happened to your cavalry?
Everybody for himself.
This-- this was far.
And we didn't know what to do.
We didn't know that the war was almost finished.
In a couple of days, the war was finished.
And the Polish vanished.
They-- most of the other officers, there went to Romania.
You know about Romania.
They went to Romania.
And we didn't know nothing, where to go or what.
We didn't have nothing to eat.
They left everybody like this.
Then we had to go-- everybody had to go-- we're going home?
That's what we were thinking.
Where should we go?
We-- we started.
And then they started collecting the Poles, Polish soldiers.
Then we went to a place.
We were over-- over water or something.
And over there, all of a sudden we see Russian soldiers.
What happened?
They divided Poland between the Germans and the Russians.
And we went to the East.
Because Vilna was to the East, you know.
And then you had to go north.
And all of a sudden, the Russian came in.
We didn't know nothing about it.
We'll continue on the next tape.
